title: The Righteous Mind: Why Good People Are Divided by Politics and Religion
subtitle: A landmark exploration of moral psychology — why our gut feelings about right and wrong drive political and religious divisions, and why each side has something important to say.
author: Jonathan Haidt
themes: The primacy of intuition over reason in moral judgment
Moral foundations as innate taste buds of the righteous mind
The rider-elephant metaphor for conscious vs automatic processing
Group selection and the evolution of human cooperation
How morality binds groups together and blinds them to other perspectives
The conservative advantage in engaging all six moral foundations
Religion as an adaptive binding mechanism
awards: New York Times Notable Book of 2012
Foreign Policy Top Global Thinkers (Jonathan Haidt, 2012)
Prospect Top World Thinkers (Jonathan Haidt, multiple years)
excerpt: Moral judgments arise not from reason but from gut feelings. We think we are prosecutors defending a case, but we are actually press secretaries justifying intuitions we arrived at before we knew we had them. The righteous mind was designed for groupish righteousness — to bind us into teams and blind us to the truth.
